title: Alps

infobox.value: [List of mountain ranges](/wiki/List_of_mountain_ranges "List of mountain ranges")

* *Alpes* ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language")) * *Alpen* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language")) * *Alpi* ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language")) * *Alps* ([Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language")) * *Alpe* ([Slovene](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language")) * (not including numerous dialects)

([/ælps/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[a]](#cite_note-2) are some of the highest and most extensive [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"),[[b]](#cite_note-3)[[2]](#cite_note-4) stretching approximately 1,200 km (750 mi) across several [Alpine countries](/wiki/Alpine_states "Alpine states") (from west to east): [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia").[[3]](#cite_note-5)

The **Alps** ([/ælps/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[a]](#cite_note-2) are some of the highest and most extensive [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"),[[b]](#cite_note-3)[[2]](#cite_note-4) stretching approximately 1,200 km (750 mi) across several [Alpine countries](/wiki/Alpine_states "Alpine states") (from west to east): [Monaco](/wiki/Monaco "Monaco"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia").[[3]](#cite_note-5)

The Alpine arch extends from [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") on the western [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") to [Trieste](/wiki/Trieste "Trieste") on the [Adriatic](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") and [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") at the beginning of the [Pannonian Basin](/wiki/Pannonian_Basin "Pannonian Basin"). The mountains were formed over tens of millions of years as the African and Eurasian [tectonic plates](/wiki/Tectonic_plate "Tectonic plate") collided. Extreme shortening caused by the event resulted in marine [sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock") rising by [thrusting](/wiki/Thrust_fault "Thrust fault") and [folding](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") into high mountain peaks such as [Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") and the [Matterhorn](/wiki/Matterhorn "Matterhorn"). The Alpine region area contains 82 peaks higher than [4,000 m (13,000 ft)](/wiki/List_of_Alpine_four-thousanders "List of Alpine four-thousanders"). The altitude and size of the range affect the climate in Europe; in the mountains, precipitation levels vary greatly and climatic conditions consist of distinct zones.

[![Massif du Mont-Blanc seen from Tête du Vuargne in commune of Verchaix, Haute-Savoie, France](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Massif_du_Mont-Blanc_from_Tete_du_Vuargne_%282%29.jpg/500px-Massif_du_Mont-Blanc_from_Tete_du_Vuargne_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Massif_du_Mont-Blanc_from_Tete_du_Vuargne_(2).jpg)

The [Mont Blanc massif](/wiki/Mont_Blanc_massif "Mont Blanc massif"), the highest range of the Alps

Evidence of human habitation in the Alps goes back to the [Palaeolithic](/wiki/Palaeolithic "Palaeolithic") era. A [mummified man ("Ötzi")](/wiki/%C3%96tzi "Ötzi"), determined to be 5,000 years old, was discovered on a glacier at the Austrian–Italian border in 1991.[[4]](#cite_note-otzi-6) By the 6th century BC, the Celtic [La Tène culture](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture") was well established. [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") notably crossed the Alps with a herd of elephants, and the [Romans](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") had settlements in the region. In 1800, [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") crossed one of the mountain passes with an army of 40,000. The 18th and 19th centuries saw an influx of naturalists, writers, and artists, in particular, the [Romanticists](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism"), followed by the [Golden Age of Alpinism](/wiki/Golden_age_of_alpinism "Golden age of alpinism") as mountaineers began to ascend the peaks of the Alps.

The Alpine region has a strong cultural identity. Traditional practices such as farming, cheese making, and woodworking still thrive in Alpine villages. However, the tourist industry began to grow early in the 20th century and expanded significantly after World War II, eventually becoming the dominant industry by the end of the century. The [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") have been hosted in the Swiss, French, Italian, Austrian and German Alps. As of 2010,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit) the region is home to 14 million people and has 120 million annual visitors.[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7)

## Etymology and toponymy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology and toponymy")]

See also: [Albion § Etymology](/wiki/Albion#Etymology "Albion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Bildstock_an_der_Alpe_Bardughe.jpg/330px-Bildstock_an_der_Alpe_Bardughe.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bildstock_an_der_Alpe_Bardughe.jpg)

An "Alp" refers to a high elevation pasture frequented only in summer. It often includes several huts and small places of worship (here the Alpe Bardughè in [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino")).

The English word *Alps* comes from the Latin *Alpes*.

The Latin word *[Alpes](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:Alpes "wikt:en:Alpes")* could possibly come from the adjective *[albus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:albus "wikt:en:albus")*[[6]](#cite_note-etymonline.com-8) ("white"), or could possibly come from the Greek goddess [Alphito](/wiki/Alphito "Alphito"), whose name is related to *alphita*, the "white flour"; *[alphos](/wiki/Alphos "Alphos")*, a dull white leprosy; and finally the [Proto-Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-European "Proto-Indo-European") word [\*albʰós](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%92%80%A0%F0%92%89%BA%F0%92%80%B8#Hittite "wikt:𒀠𒉺𒀸"). Similarly, the river god [Alpheus](/wiki/Alpheus_(deity) "Alpheus (deity)") is also supposed to derive from the Greek *alphos* and means whitish.[[7]](#cite_note-9)

In his commentary on the *Aeneid* of [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), the late fourth-century grammarian [Maurus Servius Honoratus](/wiki/Maurus_Servius_Honoratus "Maurus Servius Honoratus") says that all high mountains are called *Alpes* by Celts.[[8]](#cite_note-10)

According to the *Oxford English Dictionary*, the Latin *Alpes* might derive from a [pre-Indo-European](/wiki/Pre-Indo-European_languages "Pre-Indo-European languages") word \**alb* "hill"; "Albania" is a related derivation. Albania, the name of the region known as the country of [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), has been used as a name for several mountainous areas across Europe.

In [Roman times](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), "Albania" was a name for the eastern [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), while in the English languages "Albania" (or "Albany") was occasionally used as a name for [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"),[[9]](#cite_note-11) although it is more likely derived from the Latin word *[albus](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/en:albus "wikt:en:albus")*,[[6]](#cite_note-etymonline.com-8) the colour white.

In modern languages the term *alp*, *alm*, *albe* or *alpe* refers to [grazing pastures](/wiki/Transhumance_in_the_Alps "Transhumance in the Alps") in the alpine regions below the glaciers, not the peaks.[[10]](#cite_note-Schmid93-12)

An *alp* refers to a high mountain pasture, typically near or above the [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line"), where cows and other livestock are taken to be grazed during the summer months and where huts and hay barns can be found, sometimes constituting tiny hamlets. Therefore, the term "the Alps", as a reference to the mountains, is a misnomer.[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13)[[12]](#cite_note-Fleming_4-14) The term for the mountain peaks varies by nation and language: words such as *Horn*, *Kogel*, *Kopf*, *Gipfel*, *Spitze*, *Stock*, and *Berg* are used in German-speaking regions; *Mont*, *Pic*, *Tête*, *Pointe*, *Dent*, *Roche*, and *Aiguille* in French-speaking regions; and *Monte*, *Picco*, *Corno*, *Punta*, *Pizzo*, or *Cima* in Italian-speaking regions.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of the Alps](/wiki/Geography_of_the_Alps "Geography of the Alps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Alps_with_borders.jpg/330px-Alps_with_borders.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alps_with_borders.jpg)

The Alps extend in an arc from France in the south and west to Slovenia in the east, and from Monaco in the south to Germany in the north.

The Alps are a crescent shaped geographic feature of central Europe that ranges in an 800 km (500 mi) arc (curved line) from east to west and is 200 km (120 mi) in width. The mean height of the mountain peaks is 2.5 km (1.6 mi).[[14]](#cite_note-Ceben_22–24-16) The range stretches from the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") north above the [Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)") basin, extending through France from [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble"), and stretching eastward through mid and southern Switzerland. The range continues onward toward [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), Austria, and southeast to the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") and [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia").[[15]](#cite_note-Chatré9-17)[[16]](#cite_note-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Beattie_xii–xiii-19) Depending on the definitions used, the [Alpokalja](/wiki/Alpokalja "Alpokalja") foothills in western [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") may also qualify as part of the Alps.[[18]](#cite_note-20)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

To the south it dips into northern Italy and to the north extends to the southern border of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") in Germany.[[17]](#cite_note-Beattie_xii–xiii-19) In areas like [Chiasso](/wiki/Chiasso "Chiasso"), Switzerland, and [Allgäu](/wiki/Allg%C3%A4u "Allgäu"), Bavaria, the demarcation between the mountain range and the flatlands is clear; in other places such as [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), it is less clear.

The Alps are found in the following countries: Austria (28.7% of the range's area), Italy (27.2%), France (21.4%), Switzerland (13.2%), Germany (5.8%), Slovenia (3.6%), Liechtenstein (0.08%) and Monaco (0.001%).[[19]](#cite_note-21)

The highest portion of the range is divided by the glacial trough of the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône") valley, from [Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc "Mont Blanc") to the [Matterhorn](/wiki/Matterhorn "Matterhorn") and [Monte Rosa](/wiki/Monte_Rosa "Monte Rosa") on the southern side, and the [Bernese Alps](/wiki/Bernese_Alps "Bernese Alps") on the northern. The peaks in the easterly portion of the range, in Austria and Slovenia, are smaller than those in the central and western portions.[[17]](#cite_note-Beattie_xii–xiii-19)

The variances in nomenclature in the region spanned by the Alps make classification of the mountains and subregions difficult, but a general classification is that of the [Eastern Alps](/wiki/Eastern_Alps "Eastern Alps") and [Western Alps](/wiki/Western_Alps "Western Alps") with the divide between the two occurring in eastern Switzerland according to geologist Stefan Schmid,[[10]](#cite_note-Schmid93-12) near the [Splügen Pass](/wiki/Spl%C3%BCgen_Pass "Splügen Pass").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Lanersbach.jpg/250px-Lanersbach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lanersbach.jpg)

A typical alpine village in the [Tuxertal](/wiki/Tuxertal "Tuxertal") valley of [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(federal_state) "Tyrol (federal state)"), Austria

The highest peaks of the Western Alps and Eastern Alps, respectively, are Mont Blanc, at 4,810 m (15,780 ft),[[20]](#cite_note-22) and [Piz Bernina](/wiki/Piz_Bernina "Piz Bernina"), at 4,049 m (13,284 ft). The second-highest major peaks are the [Dufourspitze](/wiki/Dufourspitze "Dufourspitze") of the [Monte Rosa](/wiki/Monte_Rosa "Monte Rosa") massif, at 4,634 m (15,203 ft), and [Ortler](/wiki/Ortler "Ortler"),[[21]](#cite_note-23) at 3,905 m (12,810 ft), respectively.

A series of lower mountain ranges run parallel to the main chain of the Alps, including the [French Prealps](/wiki/French_Prealps "French Prealps") in France and the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains") in Switzerland and France. The secondary chain of the Alps follows the [watershed](/wiki/Drainage_divide "Drainage divide") from the Mediterranean Sea to the [Wienerwald](/wiki/Vienna_Woods "Vienna Woods"), passing over many of the highest and most well-known peaks in the Alps. From the Colle di Cadibona to [Col de Tende](/wiki/Col_de_Tende "Col de Tende") it runs westwards, before turning to the northwest and then, near the [Colle della Maddalena](/wiki/Maddalena_Pass "Maddalena Pass"), to the north. Upon reaching the Swiss border, the line of the main chain heads approximately east-northeast, a heading it follows until its end near Vienna.[[22]](#cite_note-24)

The northeast end of the Alpine arc, directly on the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), which flows into the Black Sea, is the [Leopoldsberg](/wiki/Leopoldsberg "Leopoldsberg") near Vienna. In contrast, the southeastern part of the Alps ends on the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") in the area around Trieste towards [Duino](/wiki/Duino "Duino") and [Barcola](/wiki/Barcola "Barcola").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

### Passes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Passes")]

Main article: [Principal passes of the Alps](/wiki/Principal_passes_of_the_Alps "Principal passes of the Alps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Andermatt-Teufelsbruecke.jpg/250px-Andermatt-Teufelsbruecke.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andermatt-Teufelsbruecke.jpg)

*[Teufelsbrücke](/wiki/Teufelsbr%C3%BCcke "Teufelsbrücke")* (Devil's Bridge) on the route to the [St Gotthard Pass](/wiki/St_Gotthard_Pass "St Gotthard Pass"); the currently used bridge from 1958 over the first drivable bridge from 1830

Crossing routes by foot, road, or train are known as *passes*, and usually consist of depressions in the mountains in which a valley leads from the plains and hilly pre-mountainous zones.[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoolidgeLakeKnox1911740-26)

The Alps have been crossed for explorations, commerce (historical: salt, wine, grain, textiles, and metals), military purposes, political reasons, religious and cultural motivations, and migrations for thousands of years. For a thousands of years these routes had been footpaths only. Many had been used by explorers of pre-civilised cultures at least since Stone Ages. The oldest known human mummy, the [Ötzi](/wiki/%C3%96tzi "Ötzi"), has been found 1991 on the [Tisenjoch](/w/index.php?title=Tisenjoch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tisenjoch (page does not exist)") on the Austrian-Italian border; he has been killed there about 5300 years ago. The Romans built several Roman Roads over several mountain passes in the Alps in order to control Roman territory west, north, north-east of their home base. Usually, they were also used by animal drawn carts.

Merchant traffic was supported by [pack animals](/wiki/Pack_animal "Pack animal") such as mules, horses, and even elephants (c.f. [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal")) usually on footpaths only. Already during the Roman Empire, but again in the late Middle Ages heavy carts and [sleighs](/wiki/Sleigh "Sleigh") were in use on drivable roads over Alpine passes.[[25]](#cite_note-The_Alps:_An_Environmental_History-27)

In the medieval period, [hospices](/wiki/Hospices "Hospices") were established by religious orders at the summits of many of the main passes.[[12]](#cite_note-Fleming_4-14)

Nowadays, the most important passes are the [Col de l'Iseran](/wiki/Col_de_l%27Iseran "Col de l'Iseran") (the highest), the [Col Agnel](/wiki/Col_Agnel "Col Agnel"), the [Brenner Pass](/wiki/Brenner_Pass "Brenner Pass"), the [Mont Cenis Pass](/wiki/Mont_Cenis "Mont Cenis"), the [Great St. Bernard Pass](/wiki/Great_St._Bernard_Pass "Great St. Bernard Pass"), the [Col de Tende](/wiki/Col_de_Tende "Col de Tende"), the [Saint Gotthard Pass](/wiki/Saint_Gotthard_Pass "Saint Gotthard Pass"), the [Semmering Pass](/wiki/Semmering_Pass "Semmering Pass"), the [Simplon Pass](/wiki/Simplon_Pass "Simplon Pass"), and the [Stelvio Pass](/wiki/Stelvio_Pass "Stelvio Pass").[[26]](#cite_note-Britannica-28)

Crossing the Italian-Austrian border, the Brenner Pass separates the [Ötztal Alps](/wiki/%C3%96tztal_Alps "Ötztal Alps") and [Zillertal Alps](/wiki/Zillertal_Alps "Zillertal Alps") and has been in use as a regular trading route since the Roman Times, and again since 14th century. The lowest of the Alpine passes at 984 m (3,228 ft) is the Semmering Pass crossing from [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria") to [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"). It has been in continuous use as a trade route since the 12th century, instead of the Roman Road over the Wechsel Pass. A railroad with a tunnel 1.434 km (0.891 mi) long was built along the route of the pass in the mid-19th century.

With a summit of 2,469 m (8,100 ft), the Great St Bernard Pass is one of the highest in the Alps, crossing the Italian-Swiss border east of the Pennine Alps along the flanks of Mont Blanc. The pass was already used by the Romans to control north-western provinces, and later by [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte") to cross 40,000 troops in 1800.[[27]](#cite_note-switz-29)

The [Mont Cenis](/wiki/Mont_Cenis "Mont Cenis") pass has been a major commercial and military road between Western Europe and Italy. The pass was crossed by many troops on their way to the Italian peninsula. From [Constantine I](/wiki/Constantine_I_(emperor) "Constantine I (emperor)"), [Pepin the Short](/wiki/Pepin_the_Short "Pepin the Short") and [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") to [Henry IV](/wiki/Henry_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Henry IV, Holy Roman Emperor"), Napoléon and more recently the German [Gebirgsjägers](/wiki/Gebirgsj%C3%A4ger "Gebirgsjäger") during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[28]](#cite_note-30)

In the 19th century, the principal passes of the Alps were modernised by engineers to speed up passenger and freight transport.[[25]](#cite_note-The_Alps:_An_Environmental_History-27) The Mont Cenis Pass has been supplanted by the [Fréjus Rail Tunnel](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9jus_Rail_Tunnel "Fréjus Rail Tunnel") opening in 1871, while the [Fréjus Road Tunnel](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9jus_Road_Tunnel "Fréjus Road Tunnel") opened 1980.[[29]](#cite_note-31) Railway lines could not be built in the Alps without tunnels and bridges. Apart from the Mont Cenis Railway Tunnel the [Semmering Railway Tunnel](/wiki/Semmering_railway "Semmering railway") and the [St Gotthard Railway Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Tunnel "Gotthard Tunnel") were built between 1872 and 1882. By the early 20th century eight trans-alpine railway lines had been put into operation.[[25]](#cite_note-The_Alps:_An_Environmental_History-27)

The Saint Gotthard Pass crosses from [Central Switzerland](/wiki/Central_Switzerland "Central Switzerland") to [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino "Ticino"). In 1882 the 15 km-long (9.3 mi) [Saint Gotthard Railway Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Tunnel "Gotthard Tunnel") was opened connecting [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne") in Switzerland, with [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan") in Italy. 98 years later followed the [Gotthard Road Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Road_Tunnel "Gotthard Road Tunnel") (16.9 km (10.5 mi) long) connecting the [A2 motorway](/wiki/A2_motorway_(Switzerland) "A2 motorway (Switzerland)") in [Göschenen](/wiki/G%C3%B6schenen "Göschenen") on the north side with [Airolo](/wiki/Airolo "Airolo") on the south side, exactly like the railway tunnel.

On 1 June 2016 the world's longest railway tunnel, the [Gotthard Base Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Base_Tunnel "Gotthard Base Tunnel"), was opened, which connects [Erstfeld](/wiki/Erstfeld "Erstfeld") in [canton of Uri](/wiki/Canton_of_Uri "Canton of Uri") with [Bodio](/wiki/Bodio "Bodio") in [canton of Ticino](/wiki/Canton_of_Ticino "Canton of Ticino") by two single tubes of 57.1 km (35.5 mi).[[30]](#cite_note-32) It is the first tunnel that traverses the Alps on a flat route.[[31]](#cite_note-33) From 11 December 2016, it has been part of the regular railway timetable and used hourly as standard ride between [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel")/[Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne")/[Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich") and [Bellinzona](/wiki/Bellinzona "Bellinzona")/[Lugano](/wiki/Lugano "Lugano")/[Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan").[[32]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Stelvio_Pass_Bormio_side_2.jpg/960px-Stelvio_Pass_Bormio_side_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stelvio_Pass_Bormio_side_2.jpg "Stelvio Pass (2,757 m (9,045 ft)) is the highest paved mountain pass in the Eastern Alps, and the second highest in the Alps.")

[Stelvio Pass](/wiki/Stelvio_Pass "Stelvio Pass") (2,757 m (9,045 ft)) is the [highest paved](/wiki/List_of_highest_paved_roads_in_the_Alps "List of highest paved roads in the Alps") mountain pass in the [Eastern Alps](/wiki/Eastern_Alps "Eastern Alps"), and the second highest in the Alps.

The highest drivable pass in the alps is the [Col de l'Iseran](/wiki/Col_de_l%27Iseran "Col de l'Iseran") in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") (France) at 2,770 m (9,088 ft), followed by the Stelvio Pass in northern Italy at 2,757 m (9,045 ft); the road was built in the 1820s.[[26]](#cite_note-Britannica-28)

### Highest mountains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Highest mountains")]

See also: [List of Alpine four-thousanders](/wiki/List_of_Alpine_four-thousanders "List of Alpine four-thousanders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/M%C3%A4nnlichen.jpg/500px-M%C3%A4nnlichen.jpg)](/wiki/File:M%C3%A4nnlichen.jpg)

The [Eiger](/wiki/Eiger "Eiger") (shown along with the [Mönch](/wiki/M%C3%B6nch "Mönch") and the [Jungfrau](/wiki/Jungfrau "Jungfrau")) has the tallest north face in the Alps.

The [Union Internationale des Associations d'Alpinisme](/wiki/Union_Internationale_des_Associations_d%27Alpinisme "Union Internationale des Associations d'Alpinisme") (UIAA) has defined a list of 82 "official" Alpine summits that reach at least 4,000 m (13,123 ft).[[33]](#cite_note-35) The list includes not only mountains, but also subpeaks with little [prominence](/wiki/Topographic_prominence "Topographic prominence") that are considered important [mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering") objectives. Below are listed the 29 "four-thousanders" with at least 300 m (984 ft) of prominence.

While Mont Blanc was first climbed in 1786 and the Jungfrau in 1811, most of the Alpine four-thousanders were climbed during the second half of the 19th century, notably [Piz Bernina](/wiki/Piz_Bernina "Piz Bernina") (1850), the [Dom](/wiki/Dom_(mountain) "Dom (mountain)") (1858), the [Grand Combin](/wiki/Grand_Combin "Grand Combin") (1859), the [Weisshorn](/wiki/Weisshorn "Weisshorn") (1861) and the [Barre des Écrins](/wiki/Barre_des_%C3%89crins "Barre des Écrins") (1864); the ascent of the Matterhorn in 1865 marked the end of the [golden age of alpinism](/wiki/Golden_age_of_alpinism "Golden age of alpinism"). [Karl Blodig](/wiki/Karl_Blodig "Karl Blodig") (1859–1956) was among the first to successfully climb all the major 4,000 m peaks. He completed his series of ascents in 1911.[[34]](#cite_note-36) Many of the big Alpine three-thousanders were climbed in the early 19th century, notably the [Grossglockner](/wiki/Grossglockner "Grossglockner") (1800) and the [Ortler](/wiki/Ortler "Ortler") (1804), although some of them were climbed only much later, such at [Mont Pelvoux](/wiki/Mont_Pelvoux "Mont Pelvoux") (1848), [Monte Viso](/wiki/Monte_Viso "Monte Viso") (1861) and [La Meije](/wiki/La_Meije "La Meije") (1877).

The first British Mont Blanc ascent by a man was in 1788; the first ascent by a woman was in 1808. By the mid-1850s Swiss mountaineers had ascended most of the peaks and were eagerly sought as mountain guides. [Edward Whymper](/wiki/Edward_Whymper "Edward Whymper") reached the top of the Matterhorn in 1865 (after seven attempts), and in 1938 the last of the six [great north faces of the Alps](/wiki/Great_north_faces_of_the_Alps "Great north faces of the Alps") was climbed with the first ascent of the Eiger *Nordwand* (north face of the Eiger).[[35]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff197ff-37)

### Rivers and lakes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rivers and lakes")]

Main article: [List of valleys of the Alps](/wiki/List_of_valleys_of_the_Alps "List of valleys of the Alps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Altstadt_von_Malcesine-2.jpg/250px-Altstadt_von_Malcesine-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Altstadt_von_Malcesine-2.jpg)

[Southern pre-alpine lakes](/wiki/Italian_Lakes "Italian Lakes") like [Lake Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda") are characterised by warmer [microclimates](/wiki/Microclimate "Microclimate") than the surrounding areas.

The Alps provide lowland Europe with drinking water, irrigation, and hydroelectric power.[[37]](#cite_note-39) Although the area is only about 11% of the surface area of Europe, the Alps provide up to 90% of water to lowland Europe, particularly to arid areas and during the summer months. Cities such as Milan depend on 80% of water from Alpine runoff.[[15]](#cite_note-Chatré9-17)[[38]](#cite_note-Benniston_et_al._2011,_1-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41) Water from the rivers is used in at least 550 [hydroelectricity](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") power plants, considering only those producing at least 10MW of electricity.[[40]](#cite_note-42)

Major European rivers flow from the Alps, such as the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), the [Rhône](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne "Rhône"), the [Ticino](/wiki/Ticino_(river) "Ticino (river)")->[Po](/wiki/Po_(river) "Po (river)"), and the [Inn](/wiki/Inn_(river) "Inn (river)")->[Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), all of which have headwaters in the Alps and flow into neighbouring countries, finally emptying into the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the Mediterranean Sea, the Adriatic Sea and the [Black Sea](/wiki/Black_Sea "Black Sea"). Other rivers such as the Danube have major tributaries flowing into them that originate in the Alps.[[15]](#cite_note-Chatré9-17)

The Rhône is second to the [Nile](/wiki/Nile "Nile") as a freshwater source to the Mediterranean Sea; the river begins as glacial meltwater, flows into [Lake Geneva](/wiki/Lake_Geneva "Lake Geneva"), and from there to France where one of its uses is to cool nuclear power plants.[[41]](#cite_note-Benniston_et_al._2011,_3-43) The Rhine originates in a 30 km2 (12 sq mi) area in Switzerland and represents almost 60% of water exported from the country.[[41]](#cite_note-Benniston_et_al._2011,_3-43) Tributary valleys, some of which are complicated, channel water to the main valleys which can experience flooding during the snowmelt season when rapid runoff causes debris torrents and swollen rivers.[[42]](#cite_note-Ceben_1998,_31-44)

The rivers flow through lakes, such as Lake Geneva, a crescent-shaped lake crossing the Swiss border with Lausanne on the Swiss side and the town of [Evian-les-Bains](/wiki/Evian-les-Bains "Evian-les-Bains") on the French side. In Germany, the medieval [St. Bartholomew's chapel](/wiki/St._Bartholomew%27s_Church,_Berchtesgaden "St. Bartholomew's Church, Berchtesgaden") was built on the south side of the [Königssee](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigssee "Königssee"), accessible only by boat or by climbing over the abutting peaks.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Additionally, the Alps have led to the creation of large lakes in Italy. For instance, the [Sarca](/wiki/Sarca "Sarca"), the [primary inflow](/wiki/Inflow_(hydrology) "Inflow (hydrology)") of [Lake Garda](/wiki/Lake_Garda "Lake Garda"), originates in the Italian Alps.[[44]](#cite_note-EnBrit-46) The [Italian Lakes](/wiki/Italian_Lakes "Italian Lakes") are a popular tourist destination since the Roman Era for their mild climate.

Scientists have been studying the [impact of climate change](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change "Effects of climate change") and water use. For example, each year more water is diverted from rivers for [snowmaking](/wiki/Snowmaking "Snowmaking") in the ski resorts, the effect of which is yet unknown. Furthermore, the steady decrease of glaciated areas for about 150 years—vastly accelerated during the last 30–50 years—combined with an increasingly succession of winters with lower-than-expected precipitation (snow) due to much warmer winters will have a non-negligible future impact on the water storage in the Alps, namely glacier, and consequently on rivers in the Alps as well as an effect on the water availability to the lowlands of many European countries.[[38]](#cite_note-Benniston_et_al._2011,_1-40)[[45]](#cite_note-47)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Climate")]

Main articles: [Climate of the Alps](/wiki/Climate_of_the_Alps "Climate of the Alps") and [High Alps](/wiki/High_Alps "High Alps")

The Alps are a classic example of what happens when a [temperate](/wiki/Temperateness "Temperateness") area at lower altitude gives way to higher-elevation terrain. Elevations around the world that have cold climates similar to those of the [polar regions](/wiki/Polar_region "Polar region") have been called [Alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate"). A rise from sea level into the upper regions of the atmosphere causes the temperature to decrease (see [adiabatic lapse rate](/wiki/Adiabatic_lapse_rate "Adiabatic lapse rate")). The effect of mountain chains on prevailing winds is to carry warm air belonging to the lower region into an upper zone, where it expands in volume at the cost of a proportionate loss of temperature, often accompanied by precipitation in the form of snow or rain.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoolidgeLakeKnox1911737-48) The height of the Alps is sufficient to divide the weather patterns in Europe into a wet north and dry south because moisture is sucked from the air as it flows over the high peaks.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Aletschgletscher_mit_Pinus_cembra1.jpg/250px-Aletschgletscher_mit_Pinus_cembra1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aletschgletscher_mit_Pinus_cembra1.jpg)

The [Aletsch Glacier](/wiki/Aletsch_Glacier "Aletsch Glacier") with pine trees growing on the hillside (2007; the surface is 180 m (590 ft) lower than 150 years ago; and even 20 m (66 ft) lower ten years later!)

The severe weather in the Alps has been studied since the 18th century; particularly the weather patterns such as the seasonal [foehn wind](/wiki/Foehn_wind "Foehn wind"). Numerous weather stations were placed in the mountains early in the early 20th century, providing continuous data for climatologists.[[14]](#cite_note-Ceben_22–24-16) Some of the valleys are quite arid such as the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") in Italy, the [Maurienne](/wiki/Maurienne "Maurienne") in France, the [Valais](/wiki/Valais "Valais") in Switzerland, and northern [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(federal_state) "Tyrol (federal state)").[[14]](#cite_note-Ceben_22–24-16)

The areas that are not arid and receive high precipitation experience periodic flooding from rapid snowmelt and runoff.[[42]](#cite_note-Ceben_1998,_31-44) The mean precipitation in the Alps ranges from a low of 2,600 mm (100 in) per year to 3,600 mm (140 in) per year, with the higher levels occurring at high altitudes. At altitudes between 1,000 and 3,000 m (3,300 and 9,800 ft), snowfall begins in November and accumulates through to April or May when the melt begins. [Snow lines](/wiki/Snow_line "Snow line") vary from 2,400 to 3,000 m (7,900 to 9,800 ft), above which the snow is permanent and the temperatures hover around the freezing point even during July and August. High-water levels in streams and rivers peak in June and July when the snow is still melting at the higher altitudes.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

The Alps are split into five climatic zones, each with different vegetation. The climate, plant life, and animal life vary among the different sections or zones of the mountains. The lowest zone is the colline zone, which exists between 500 and 1,000 m (1,600 and 3,300 ft), depending on the location. The [montane](/wiki/Montane "Montane") zone extends from 800 to 1,700 m (2,600 to 5,600 ft), followed by the sub-Alpine zone from 1,600 to 2,400 m (5,200 to 7,900 ft). The Alpine zone, extending from [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line") to the snow line, is followed by the glacial zone, which covers the glaciated areas of the mountain. Climatic conditions show variances within the same zones; for example, weather conditions at the head of a mountain valley, extending directly from the peaks, are colder and more severe than those at the mouth of a valley which tend to be less severe and receive less snowfall.[[49]](#cite_note-51)

#### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Climate change")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Duration_of_the_yearly_snow_cover_ring-width_reconstruction_together_with_modelled_record_for_the_Alps.webp/250px-Duration_of_the_yearly_snow_cover_ring-width_reconstruction_together_with_modelled_record_for_the_Alps.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Duration_of_the_yearly_snow_cover_ring-width_reconstruction_together_with_modelled_record_for_the_Alps.webp)

Shrinkage of snow cover duration, starting ~end of the 19th century[[50]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41558-022-01575-3-52)

Various models of [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") have been projected into the 22nd century for the Alps, with an expectation that a trend toward increased temperatures will have an effect on [snowfall, snowpack, glaciation, and river runoff](/wiki/Effects_of_climate_change#Ice_and_snow "Effects of climate change").[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-54) Significant changes, of both natural and anthropogenic origins, have already been diagnosed from observations,[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) including a 5.6% reduction per decade in snow cover duration over the last 50 years, which also highlights [climate change adaptation](/wiki/Climate_change_adaptation "Climate change adaptation") needs due to impacts on the climate and regional socio-economic activities.[[50]](#cite_note-10.1038/s41558-022-01575-3-52)

## Geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geology")]

Main articles: [Alpine orogeny](/wiki/Alpine_orogeny "Alpine orogeny") and [Geology of the Alps](/wiki/Geology_of_the_Alps "Geology of the Alps")

Important geological concepts were established as naturalists began studying the rock formations of the Alps in the 18th century. In the mid-19th century, the now-defunct idea of [geosynclines](/wiki/Geosyncline "Geosyncline") was used to explain the presence of "folded" mountain chains. This theory was replaced in the mid-20th century by the theory of [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics").[[56]](#cite_note-Graciansky1ff-58)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Nante_d%27arpanaz.jpg/330px-Nante_d%27arpanaz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nante_d%27arpanaz.jpg)

The geologic [folding](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)") seen at the Arpenaz waterfall, shown here in a mid-18th-century drawing, was noted by 18th-century geologists.[[57]](#cite_note-Graciansky5-59)

The formation of the Alps (the [Alpine orogeny](/wiki/Alpine_orogeny "Alpine orogeny")) was an episodic process that began about 300 million years ago.[[58]](#cite_note-60) In the [Paleozoic](/wiki/Paleozoic "Paleozoic") Era the [Pangaean](/wiki/Pangaea "Pangaea") [supercontinent](/wiki/Supercontinent "Supercontinent") consisted of a single [tectonic plate](/wiki/Tectonic_plate "Tectonic plate"); it broke into separate plates during the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") Era and the [Tethys](/wiki/Tethys_Ocean "Tethys Ocean") sea developed between [Laurasia](/wiki/Laurasia "Laurasia") and [Gondwana](/wiki/Gondwana "Gondwana") during the [Jurassic](/wiki/Jurassic "Jurassic") Period.[[56]](#cite_note-Graciansky1ff-58) The Tethys was later squeezed between colliding plates causing the formation of mountain ranges called the [Alpide belt](/wiki/Alpide_belt "Alpide belt"), from [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") through the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") to [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")—a process that began at the end of the [Mesozoic](/wiki/Mesozoic "Mesozoic") and continues into the present. The formation of the Alps was a segment of this orogenic process,[[56]](#cite_note-Graciansky1ff-58) caused by the collision between the [African](/wiki/African_Plate "African Plate") and the [Eurasian](/wiki/Eurasian_Plate "Eurasian Plate") plates[[59]](#cite_note-Gerrard9-61) that began in the late [Cretaceous](/wiki/Cretaceous "Cretaceous") Period.[[60]](#cite_note-Gerrard16-62)

Under extreme [compressive stresses](/wiki/Compressive_stress "Compressive stress") and pressure, marine [sedimentary rocks](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock") were uplifted, forming characteristic recumbent [folds](/wiki/Fold_(geology) "Fold (geology)"), and [thrust faults](/wiki/Thrust_fault "Thrust fault").[[61]](#cite_note-63) As the rising peaks underwent erosion, a layer of marine [flysch](/wiki/Flysch "Flysch") sediments was deposited in the [foreland basin](/wiki/Foreland_basin "Foreland basin"), and the sediments became involved in younger folds as the orogeny progressed. Coarse sediments from the continual uplift and erosion were later deposited in [foreland areas](/wiki/Molasse_basin "Molasse basin") north of the Alps.[[59]](#cite_note-Gerrard9-61) These regions in Switzerland and Bavaria are well-developed, containing classic examples of [flysch](/wiki/Flysch "Flysch"), which is [sedimentary rock](/wiki/Sedimentary_rock "Sedimentary rock") formed during mountain building.[[62]](#cite_note-Schmid102-64)

The Alpine orogeny occurred in ongoing cycles through to the Paleogene causing differences in folded structures, with a late-stage orogeny causing the development of the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains").[[63]](#cite_note-Schmid97-65) A series of tectonic events in the Triassic, Jurassic and Cretaceous periods caused different [paleogeographic](/wiki/Palaeogeography "Palaeogeography") regions.[[63]](#cite_note-Schmid97-65) The Alps are subdivided by different [lithology](/wiki/Lithology "Lithology") (rock composition) and nappe structures according to the orogenic events that affected them.[[10]](#cite_note-Schmid93-12) The geological subdivision differentiates the Western, Eastern Alps, and Southern Alps: the [Helveticum](/wiki/Helvetic_nappes "Helvetic nappes") in the north, the [Penninicum](/wiki/Penninic_nappes "Penninic nappes") and [Austroalpine system](/wiki/Austroalpine_nappes "Austroalpine nappes") in the centre and, south of the Periadriatic Seam, the [Southern Alpine system](/wiki/Southern_Alps_(geology) "Southern Alps (geology)").[[64]](#cite_note-Schmid99-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Matterhorn_from_Domh%C3%BCtte_-_2.jpg/330px-Matterhorn_from_Domh%C3%BCtte_-_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matterhorn_from_Domh%C3%BCtte_-_2.jpg)

Compressed metamorphosed Tethyan sediments and their oceanic basement are sandwiched between the tip of the [Matterhorn](/wiki/Matterhorn "Matterhorn") (Italian-Swiss border), which consists of gneisses originally part of the African plate, and the base of the peak, which is part of the Eurasian plate.[[57]](#cite_note-Graciansky5-59)

According to geologist Stefan Schmid, because the Western Alps underwent a metamorphic event in the [Cenozoic](/wiki/Cenozoic "Cenozoic") Era while the Austroalpine peaks underwent an event in the Cretaceous Period, the two areas show distinct differences in nappe formations.[[63]](#cite_note-Schmid97-65) [Flysch](/wiki/Flysch "Flysch") deposits in the Southern Alps of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") probably occurred in the Cretaceous or later.[[63]](#cite_note-Schmid97-65)

Peaks in France, Italy and Switzerland lie in the "Houillière zone", which consists of [basement](/wiki/Basement_(geology) "Basement (geology)") with sediments from the Mesozoic Era.[[64]](#cite_note-Schmid99-66) High "massifs" with external sedimentary cover are more common in the Western Alps and were affected by [Neogene](/wiki/Neogene "Neogene") Period [thin-skinned](/wiki/Thin-skinned_deformation "Thin-skinned deformation") thrusting whereas the Eastern Alps have comparatively few high peaked massifs.[[62]](#cite_note-Schmid102-64) Similarly the peaks in eastern Switzerland extending to western Austria (Helvetic nappes) consist of thin-skinned sedimentary folding that [detached](/wiki/Decollement "Decollement") from former [basement](/wiki/Basement_(geology) "Basement (geology)") rock.[[65]](#cite_note-Schmid103-67)

In simple terms, the structure of the Alps consists of layers of rock of European, African, and oceanic (Tethyan) origin.[[66]](#cite_note-Graciansky29ff-68) The bottom nappe structure is of continental European origin, above which are stacked marine sediment nappes, topped off by nappes derived from the African plate.[[67]](#cite_note-Graciansky31-69) The Matterhorn is an example of the ongoing orogeny and shows evidence of great folding. The tip of the mountain consists of [gneisses](/wiki/Gneiss "Gneiss") from the African plate; the base of the peak, below the glaciated area, consists of European basement rock. The sequence of Tethyan marine sediments and their oceanic basement is sandwiched between rock derived from the African and European plates.[[57]](#cite_note-Graciansky5-59)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Savine.JPG/960px-Savine.JPG)](/wiki/File:Savine.JPG "Haute Maurienne (Ambin and Vanoise massifs) and its exposed crystalline basement made of high-pressure subduction rocks such as blueschist and metaquartzite (picture taken at 2,400 metres or 7,900 feet)")

[Haute Maurienne](/wiki/Maurienne "Maurienne") (Ambin and Vanoise massifs) and its exposed crystalline basement made of high-pressure subduction rocks such as [blueschist](/wiki/Blueschist "Blueschist") and [metaquartzite](/wiki/Quartzite "Quartzite") (picture taken at 2,400 metres or 7,900 feet)

The core regions of the Alpine orogenic belt have been folded and fractured in such a manner that erosion produced the characteristic steep vertical peaks of the [Swiss Alps](/wiki/Swiss_Alps "Swiss Alps") that rise seemingly straight out of the foreland areas.[[60]](#cite_note-Gerrard16-62) Peaks such as Mont Blanc, the Matterhorn, and high peaks in the Pennine Alps, the Briançonnais, and [Hohe Tauern](/wiki/Hohe_Tauern "Hohe Tauern") consist of layers of rock from the various orogenies including exposures of basement rock.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Due to the ever-present geologic instability, earthquakes continue in the Alps to this day.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Typically, the largest earthquakes in the alps have been between magnitude 6 and 7 on the Richter scale.[[70]](#cite_note-72) [Geodetic](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") measurements show ongoing topographic uplift at rates of up to about 2.5 mm per year in the North, Western and Central Alps, and at ~1 mm per year in the Eastern and South-Western Alps.[[71]](#cite_note-Sternai-2019-73) The underlying mechanisms that jointly drive the present-day uplift pattern are the isostatic rebound due to the melting of the last glacial maximum ice-cap or long-term erosion, detachment of the Western Alpine subducting slab, mantle convection as well as ongoing horizontal convergence between Africa and Europe, but their relative contributions to the uplift of the Alps are difficult to quantify and likely to vary significantly in space and time.[[71]](#cite_note-Sternai-2019-73)

### Minerals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Minerals")]

The Alps are a source of minerals that have been mined for thousands of years. In the 8th to 6th centuries, BC during the [Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture"), Celtic tribes mined copper; later the Romans mined gold for coins in the [Bad Gastein](/wiki/Bad_Gastein "Bad Gastein") area. [Erzberg](/wiki/Erzberg "Erzberg") in [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria") furnishes high-quality iron ore for the steel industry. Crystals, such as [cinnabar](/wiki/Cinnabar "Cinnabar"), [amethyst](/wiki/Amethyst "Amethyst"), and [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz"), are found throughout much of the Alpine region. The cinnabar deposits in Slovenia are a notable source of cinnabar pigments.[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Alpine crystals have been studied and collected for hundreds of years and began to be classified in the 18th century. [Leonhard Euler](/wiki/Leonhard_Euler "Leonhard Euler") studied the shapes of crystals, and by the 19th-century crystal hunting was common in Alpine regions. David Friedrich Wiser amassed a collection of 8000 crystals that he studied and documented. In the 20th century Robert Parker wrote a well-known work about the rock crystals of the Swiss Alps; at the same period a commission was established to control and standardise the naming of Alpine minerals.[[73]](#cite_note-75)

### Glaciers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Glaciers")]

See also: [List of glaciers in Switzerland](/wiki/List_of_glaciers_in_Switzerland "List of glaciers in Switzerland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/9b/Illustration_of_the_Glacier_System_of_the_Alps_by_Alexander_Keith_Johnston_1848.png/500px-Illustration_of_the_Glacier_System_of_the_Alps_by_Alexander_Keith_Johnston_1848.png)](/wiki/File:Illustration_of_the_Glacier_System_of_the_Alps_by_Alexander_Keith_Johnston_1848.png)

This illustration of the glacier systems of the [Mont Blanc massif](/wiki/Mont_Blanc_massif "Mont Blanc massif") by [Alexander Keith Johnston](/wiki/Alexander_Keith_Johnston_(1804%E2%80%931871) "Alexander Keith Johnston (1804–1871)") was first published 1848 in *The Physical Atlas*.

In the [Miocene](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene") Epoch the mountains underwent severe erosion because of glaciation,[[60]](#cite_note-Gerrard16-62) which was noted in the mid-19th century by naturalist [Louis Agassiz](/wiki/Louis_Agassiz "Louis Agassiz") who presented a paper proclaiming the Alps were covered in ice at various intervals—a theory he formed when studying rocks near his [Neuchâtel](/wiki/Neuch%C3%A2tel "Neuchâtel") home which he believed originated to the west in the Bernese Oberland. Because of his work he came to be known as the "father of the ice-age concept" although other naturalists before him put forth similar ideas.[[74]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff63ff-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Aletschgebiet_aus_dem_Flugzeug.jpg/250px-Aletschgebiet_aus_dem_Flugzeug.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aletschgebiet_aus_dem_Flugzeug.jpg)

[Louis Agassiz](/wiki/Louis_Agassiz "Louis Agassiz")'s studies of the [Unteraar Glacier](/wiki/Unteraar_Glacier "Unteraar Glacier") in the 1840s showed that it moved at 100 m (328 ft) per year.[[74]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff63ff-76)

Agassiz studied glacier movement in the 1840s at the [Unteraar Glacier](/wiki/Unteraar_Glacier "Unteraar Glacier") where he found the glacier moved 100 m (328 ft) per year, more rapidly in the middle than at the edges. His work was continued by other scientists and now a permanent laboratory exists inside a glacier under the [Jungfraujoch](/wiki/Jungfraujoch "Jungfraujoch"), devoted exclusively to the study of Alpine glaciers.[[74]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff63ff-76)

Glaciers pick up rocks and sediment with them as they flow. This causes erosion and the formation of valleys over time. The [Inn](/wiki/Inn_(river) "Inn (river)") valley is an example of a valley carved by glaciers during the [ice ages](/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation") with a typical terraced structure caused by erosion. Eroded rocks from the most recent ice age lie at the bottom of the valley while the top of the valley consists of erosion from earlier ice ages.[[74]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff63ff-76) Glacial valleys have characteristically steep walls (reliefs); valleys with lower reliefs and [talus slopes](/wiki/Scree "Scree") are remnants of glacial troughs or previously infilled valleys.[[75]](#cite_note-Gerrard132-77) [Moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"), piles of rock picked up during the movement of the glacier, accumulate at edges, centre, and the terminus of glaciers.[[74]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff63ff-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Jungfraujoch_img_3699.jpg/250px-Jungfraujoch_img_3699.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jungfraujoch_img_3699.jpg)

The Sphinx Tunnel connecting [Jungfraujoch railway station](/wiki/Jungfraujoch_railway_station "Jungfraujoch railway station") to the [Sphinx Observatory](/wiki/Sphinx_Observatory "Sphinx Observatory"), through a glacier at the [Jungfraujoch](/wiki/Jungfraujoch "Jungfraujoch")

Alpine glaciers can be straight rivers of ice, long sweeping rivers, spread in a fan-like shape (Piedmont glaciers), and curtains of ice that hang from vertical slopes of the mountain peaks. The stress of the movement causes the ice to break and crack loudly, perhaps explaining why the mountains were believed to be home to dragons in the medieval period. The cracking creates unpredictable and dangerous [crevasses](/wiki/Crevasse "Crevasse"), often invisible under new snowfall, which causes the greatest danger to mountaineers.[[76]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff71ff-78)

Glaciers end in ice caves (the [Rhône Glacier](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne_Glacier "Rhône Glacier")), by trailing into a lake or river, or by shedding snowmelt on a meadow. Sometimes a piece of glacier will detach or break resulting in flooding, property damage, and loss of life.[[76]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff71ff-78)

High levels of precipitation cause the glaciers to descend to [permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost") levels in some areas whereas in other, more arid regions, glaciers remain above about the 3,500 m (11,483 ft) level.[[77]](#cite_note-Gerrard78-79) The 1,817 km2 (702 sq mi) of the Alps covered by glaciers in 1876 had shrunk to 1,342 km2 (518 sq mi) by 1973, resulting in decreased river run-off levels.[[78]](#cite_note-Gerrard108-80) Forty percent of the glaciation in Austria has disappeared since 1850, and 30% of that in Switzerland.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

Although the Alpine topography shows marked glacial morphologies,[[80]](#cite_note-82) the mechanisms by which glacial reshaping occurs are unclear. [Numerical modelling](/wiki/Computer_simulation "Computer simulation") suggests that glacial erosion propagates from low elevations to high elevations leading to an early increase of local relief followed by lowering of the mean orogen elevation.[[81]](#cite_note-83)
