## Ecology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Ecology")]

Main article: [Alps conifer and mixed forests](/wiki/Alps_conifer_and_mixed_forests "Alps conifer and mixed forests")

### Flora

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Flora")]

Main article: [Flora of the Alps](/wiki/Flora_of_the_Alps "Flora of the Alps")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Gentiana_clusii03.jpg/250px-Gentiana_clusii03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gentiana_clusii03.jpg)

Stemless gentian (*[Gentiana acaulis](/wiki/Gentiana_acaulis "Gentiana acaulis")*)

Thirteen thousand species of plants have been identified in the Alpine regions.[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7) Alpine plants are grouped by habitat and soil type which can be [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") or non-calcareous. The habitats range from meadows, bogs, and woodland (deciduous and coniferous) areas to soil-less scree and [moraines](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine"), and rock faces and ridges.[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13) A natural vegetation limit with altitude is given by the presence of the chief [deciduous](/wiki/Deciduous "Deciduous") trees—oak, beech, [ash](/wiki/Ash_tree "Ash tree") and [sycamore maple](/wiki/Sycamore_maple "Sycamore maple"). These do not reach the same elevation, nor are they often found growing together, but their upper limit corresponds accurately enough to the change from a temperate to a colder climate that is further proved by a change in the presence of wild herbaceous vegetation.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoolidgeLakeKnox1911738-85) This limit usually lies about 1,200 m (3,900 ft) above the sea on the north side of the Alps, but on the southern slopes it often rises to 1,500 m (4,900 ft), sometimes even to 1,700 m (5,600 ft).[[84]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Leontopodium_alpinum1.jpg/250px-Leontopodium_alpinum1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Leontopodium_alpinum1.jpg)

Edelweiss (*[Leontopodium alpinum](/wiki/Edelweiss "Edelweiss")*)

Above the forestry, there is often a band of dwarf pine trees (*[Pinus mugo](/wiki/Pinus_mugo "Pinus mugo")*), which is in turn superseded by *Alpenrosen*, dwarf shrubs, typically *[Rhododendron ferrugineum](/wiki/Rhododendron_ferrugineum "Rhododendron ferrugineum")* (on acid soils) or *[Rhododendron hirsutum](/wiki/Rhododendron_hirsutum "Rhododendron hirsutum")* (on alkaline soils).[[85]](#cite_note-87) Although Alpenrose prefers acidic soil, the plants are found throughout the region.[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13) Above the [tree line](/wiki/Tree_line "Tree line") is the area defined as "alpine" where in the [alpine meadow](/wiki/Alpine_meadow "Alpine meadow") plants are found that have adapted well to harsh conditions of cold temperatures, aridity, and [high altitudes](/wiki/High-altitude_adaptation "High-altitude adaptation"). The alpine area fluctuates greatly because of regional fluctuations in tree lines.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

Alpine plants such as the [Alpine gentian](/wiki/Gentiana_acaulis "Gentiana acaulis") grow in abundance in areas such as the meadows above the [Lauterbrunnental](/wiki/Lauterbrunnental "Lauterbrunnental"). Gentians are named after the [Illyrian](/wiki/Illyria "Illyria") king [Gentius](/wiki/Gentius "Gentius"), and 40 species of the early-spring blooming flower grow in the Alps, in a range of 1,500 to 2,400 m (4,900 to 7,900 ft).[[87]](#cite_note-89) Writing about the gentians in Switzerland [D. H. Lawrence](/wiki/D._H._Lawrence "D. H. Lawrence") described them as "darkening the day-time, torch-like with the smoking blueness of Pluto's gloom."[[88]](#cite_note-90) Gentians tend to "appear" repeatedly as the spring blooming takes place at progressively later dates, moving from the lower altitude to the higher altitude meadows where the snow melts much later than in the valleys. On the highest rocky ledges, the spring flowers bloom in the summer.[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Naturasollieres.jpg/250px-Naturasollieres.jpg)](/wiki/File:Naturasollieres.jpg)

Preserved internal alpine forest and meadow, [Vanoise National Park](/wiki/Vanoise_National_Park "Vanoise National Park")

At these higher altitudes, the plants tend to form isolated cushions. In the Alps, several species of flowering plants have been recorded above 4,000 m (13,000 ft), including *[Ranunculus glacialis](/wiki/Ranunculus_glacialis "Ranunculus glacialis")*, *[Androsace alpina](/wiki/Androsace_alpina "Androsace alpina")* and *[Saxifraga biflora](/w/index.php?title=Saxifraga_biflora&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saxifraga biflora (page does not exist)")*. *[Eritrichium nanum](/wiki/Eritrichium_nanum "Eritrichium nanum")*, commonly known as the King of the Alps, is the most elusive of the alpine flowers, growing on rocky ridges at 2,600 to 3,750 m (8,530 to 12,300 ft).[[89]](#cite_note-91) Perhaps the best known of the alpine plants is [Edelweiss](/wiki/Edelweiss "Edelweiss") which grows in rocky areas and can be found at altitudes as low as 1,200 m (3,900 ft) and as high as 3,400 m (11,200 ft).[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13) The plants that grow at the highest altitudes have adapted to conditions by specialisation such as growing in rock screes that give protection from winds.[[90]](#cite_note-Sharp14ff-92)

The alpine meadows are also known for their distinct floral aromas during summer: the sensory landscape is often defined by the strong, spicy-sweet fragrance of [Alpine clover (*Trifolium alpinum*)](/wiki/Trifolium_alpinum "Trifolium alpinum"), a characteristic scent of the high pastures.[[91]](#cite_note-93)[[92]](#cite_note-94)

The extreme and stressful climatic conditions give way to the growth of plant species with [secondary metabolites](/wiki/Secondary_metabolites "Secondary metabolites") important for medicinal purposes. *[Origanum vulgare](/wiki/Origanum_vulgare "Origanum vulgare")*, *[Prunella vulgaris](/wiki/Prunella_vulgaris "Prunella vulgaris")*, *[Solanum nigrum](/wiki/Solanum_nigrum "Solanum nigrum")*, and *[Urtica dioica](/wiki/Urtica_dioica "Urtica dioica")* are some of the more useful medicinal species found in the Alps.[[93]](#cite_note-95) Human interference has nearly exterminated the trees in many areas, and, except for the beech forests of the Austrian Alps, forests of deciduous trees are rarely found after the extreme deforestation between the 17th and 19th centuries.[[94]](#cite_note-96) The vegetation has changed since the second half of the 20th century, as the high alpine meadows cease to be harvested for hay or used for grazing which eventually might result in a regrowth of the forest. In some areas, the modern practice of building ski runs by mechanical means has destroyed the underlying tundra from which the plant life cannot recover during the non-skiing months, whereas areas that still practice a natural *piste* type of ski slope building preserve the fragile underlayers.[[90]](#cite_note-Sharp14ff-92)

### Fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Fauna")]

The Alps are a habitat for 30,000 species of wildlife, ranging from the tiniest [snow fleas](/wiki/Snow_flea "Snow flea") to [brown bears](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear"), many of which have made adaptations to the harsh cold conditions and high altitudes to the point that some only survive in specific micro-climates either directly above or below the [snow line](/wiki/Snow_line "Snow line").[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7)[[95]](#cite_note-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Jeune_bouquetin_de_face.jpg/250px-Jeune_bouquetin_de_face.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeune_bouquetin_de_face.jpg)

Young alpine ibex. When fully grown the horns of this male will be about one metre wide.

The largest mammal to live in the highest altitudes are the [alpine ibex](/wiki/Alpine_ibex "Alpine ibex"), which have been sighted as high as 3,000 m (9,800 ft). The ibex live in caves and descend to eat the succulent alpine grasses.[[96]](#cite_note-98) Classified as [antelopes](/wiki/Antelopes "Antelopes"),[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13) [chamois](/wiki/Chamois "Chamois") are smaller than ibex and found throughout the Alps, living above the tree line and are common in the entire alpine range.[[97]](#cite_note-99) Areas of the eastern Alps are still home to brown bears. In Switzerland the [canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern") was named for the bears but the last bear is recorded as having been killed in 1792 above [Kleine Scheidegg](/wiki/Kleine_Scheidegg "Kleine Scheidegg") by three hunters from [Grindelwald](/wiki/Grindelwald "Grindelwald").[[98]](#cite_note-100)

Many rodents such as [voles](/wiki/Voles "Voles") live underground. [Marmots](/wiki/Marmots "Marmots") live almost exclusively above the tree line as high as 2,700 m (8,900 ft). They hibernate in large groups to provide warmth,[[99]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff102ff-101) and can be found in all areas of the Alps, in large colonies they build beneath the alpine pastures.[[11]](#cite_note-Reynolds43ff-13) [Golden eagles](/wiki/Golden_eagle "Golden eagle") and [bearded vultures](/wiki/Bearded_vulture "Bearded vulture") are the largest birds to be found in the Alps; they nest high on rocky ledges and can be found at altitudes of 2,400 m (7,900 ft). The most common bird is the [alpine chough](/wiki/Alpine_chough "Alpine chough") which can be found scavenging at climber's huts or the [Jungfraujoch](/wiki/Jungfraujoch "Jungfraujoch"), a high-altitude tourist destination.[[100]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff97ff-102)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Parnassius_phoebus.jpg/250px-Parnassius_phoebus.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parnassius_phoebus.jpg)

The alpine [Apollo butterfly](/wiki/Apollo_butterfly "Apollo butterfly") has adapted to alpine conditions.

Reptiles such as [adders and vipers](/wiki/Viperidae "Viperidae") live up to the snow line; because they cannot bear the cold temperatures they hibernate underground and soak up the warmth on rocky ledges.[[101]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff95ff-103) The high-altitude [Alpine salamanders](/wiki/Alpine_salamander "Alpine salamander") have adapted to living above the snow line by giving birth to fully developed young rather than laying eggs. [Brown trout](/wiki/Brown_trout "Brown trout") can be found in the streams up to the snow line.[[101]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff95ff-103) Molluscs such as the [wood snail](/wiki/Helicigona "Helicigona") live up the snow line. Popularly gathered as food, the [snails](/wiki/Snail "Snail") are now protected.[[102]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff88ff-104)

Several species of [moths](/wiki/Moth "Moth") live in the Alps, some of which are believed to have evolved in the same habitat up to 120 million years ago, long before the Alps were created. [Blue butterflies](/wiki/Lycaenidae "Lycaenidae") can commonly be seen drinking from the snowmelt; some species of blues fly as high as 1,800 m (5,900 ft).[[103]](#cite_note-105) The butterflies tend to be large, such as those from the swallowtail *[Parnassius](/wiki/Parnassius "Parnassius")* family, with a habitat that ranges to 1,800 m (5,900 ft). Twelve species of beetles have habitats up to the snow line; the most beautiful and formerly collected for its colours but now protected is *[Rosalia alpina](/wiki/Rosalia_alpina "Rosalia alpina")*.[[104]](#cite_note-106) Spiders, such as the large [wolf spider](/wiki/Wolf_spider "Wolf spider"), live above the snow line and can be seen as high as 400 m (1,300 ft). [Scorpions](/wiki/Scorpion "Scorpion") can be found in the Italian Alps.[[102]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff88ff-104)

Some of the species of moths and insects show evidence of having been indigenous to the area from as long ago as the Alpine orogeny. In [Émosson](/wiki/Lac_d%27%C3%89mosson "Lac d'Émosson") in Valais, Switzerland, dinosaur tracks were found in the 1970s, dating probably from the [Triassic](/wiki/Triassic "Triassic") Period.[[105]](#cite_note-107)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the Alps](/wiki/History_of_the_Alps "History of the Alps")

### Prehistory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Prehistory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Scena_di_duello_R6_-_Foppe_-_Nadro_%28Foto_Luca_Giarelli%29.jpg/250px-Scena_di_duello_R6_-_Foppe_-_Nadro_%28Foto_Luca_Giarelli%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scena_di_duello_R6_-_Foppe_-_Nadro_(Foto_Luca_Giarelli).jpg)

[Petroglyphs](/wiki/Petroglyphs "Petroglyphs"), [Rock Drawings in Valcamonica](/wiki/Rock_Drawings_in_Valcamonica "Rock Drawings in Valcamonica"), Italy, which was recognised by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") in 1979 and was Italy's first recognised [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Otzi-Quinson.jpg/250px-Otzi-Quinson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Otzi-Quinson.jpg)

Reconstruction of [Ötzi](/wiki/%C3%96tzi "Ötzi") mummy as shown in [Alpes-de-Haute-Provence](/wiki/Alpes-de-Haute-Provence "Alpes-de-Haute-Provence"), France. The original mummy and his remains and personal belongings are on exhibit at the [South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology](/wiki/South_Tyrol_Museum_of_Archaeology "South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology") in [Bolzano](/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), Italy.

When the ice melted after the [Würm glaciation](/wiki/W%C3%BCrm_glaciation "Würm glaciation"), [Paleolithic](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") settlements were established along the lake shores and in cave systems. Evidence of human habitation has been found in caves near the [Vercors Cave System](/wiki/Vercors_Cave_System "Vercors Cave System"), close to Grenoble and Echirolles. In [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), the [Mondsee lake](/wiki/Mondsee_(lake) "Mondsee (lake)") shows evidence of houses built on piles. Standing stones have been found in the Alpine areas of [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). About 200,000 drawings and etchings have been documented, and are known as the [Rock Drawings in Valcamonica](/wiki/Rock_Drawings_in_Valcamonica "Rock Drawings in Valcamonica").[[106]](#cite_note-Beattie25ff-108)

A mummy of a [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") human, known as [Ötzi](/wiki/%C3%96tzi "Ötzi"), was discovered on the [Similaun](/wiki/Similaun "Similaun"). His clothing lets modern people assume that he was an alpine farmer, while the location and manner of his death suggests that Ötzi was travelling.[[107]](#cite_note-Beattie21ff-109) Analysis of the [mitochondrial DNA](/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA "Mitochondrial DNA") of Ötzi, has shown that he belongs to the K1 [subclade](/wiki/Subclade "Subclade").[[108]](#cite_note-110) His remains and personal belongings are on exhibit at the [South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology](/wiki/South_Tyrol_Museum_of_Archaeology "South Tyrol Museum of Archaeology") in [Bolzano](/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), Italy.

From the 13th to the 6th century BC much of the Alps was settled by the [Germanic peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples"), [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards"), [Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni"), [Bavarii](/wiki/Bavarii "Bavarii"), and [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks").[[109]](#cite_note-111) [Celt](/wiki/Celt "Celt") tribes settled in modern-day [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") between 1500 and 1000 BC. The [Raeti](/wiki/Raeti "Raeti") lived in the eastern regions, while the west was occupied by the [Helvetii](/wiki/Helvetii "Helvetii") and the [Allobroges](/wiki/Allobroges "Allobroges") settled in the Rhône valley and in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy"). The [Ligures](/wiki/Ligures "Ligures") and [Adriatic Veneti](/wiki/Adriatic_Veneti "Adriatic Veneti") lived in [Northwest Italy](/wiki/Northwest_Italy "Northwest Italy") and [Triveneto](/wiki/Triveneto "Triveneto") respectively. The Celts mined [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt") in areas such as [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg"), where evidence was found of the [Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture").[[106]](#cite_note-Beattie25ff-108) By the 6th century BC the [La Tène culture](/wiki/La_T%C3%A8ne_culture "La Tène culture") was well established in the region,[[110]](#cite_note-112) and became known for high quality [Celtic art](/wiki/Celtic_art "Celtic art").[[111]](#cite_note-113)

Between 430 and 400 BC prolonged warfare in the Alps resulted in the devastation of agricultural land and human settlements, ultimately triggering the [enslavement](/wiki/Enslavement "Enslavement") of men, women, and children, goods had to be imported as a result. The [Etruscan civilisation](/wiki/Etruscan_civilization "Etruscan civilization") responded to raids by the [Massalia](/wiki/Massalia "Massalia") and acquired absolute control over the Alpine trade routes. Aggressors in modern-day Italy were dealt with and an alliance was formed with the Celts. The grip of the Etruscan settlements broke down, as the Roman political system expanded, so as to take control over Alpine trade routes that connected human settlements in the Alps with settlements in the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean").[[112]](#cite_note-114)

During the [Second Punic War](/wiki/Second_Punic_War "Second Punic War") in 218 BC, the [Carthage](/wiki/Carthage "Carthage") general [Hannibal](/wiki/Hannibal "Hannibal") initiated one of the most celebrated achievements of any military force in ancient warfare, recorded as [Hannibal crossing the Alps](/wiki/Hannibal_crossing_the_Alps "Hannibal crossing the Alps").[[113]](#cite_note-Lancel-115) The [Roman people](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people") built roads along the Alpine mountain passes, which continued to be used through the medieval period. Roman road markers can still be found on the Alpine mountain passes.[[114]](#cite_note-116)

During the [Gallic Wars](/wiki/Gallic_Wars "Gallic Wars") in 58 BC [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") defeated the [Helvetii](/wiki/Helvetii "Helvetii"). The [Rhaetian](/wiki/Rhaetian_people "Rhaetian people") continued to resist but their territory was eventually conquered when the Romans crossed the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") valley and defeated the [Brigantes](/wiki/Brigantes "Brigantes").[[115]](#cite_note-Beattie27ff-117) The Romans built settlements in the Alps. In towns such as [Aosta](/wiki/Aosta "Aosta"), [Martigny](/wiki/Martigny "Martigny"), [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"), and [Partenkirchen](/wiki/Partenkirchen "Partenkirchen") remains of villas, arenas, and temples have been discovered.[[116]](#cite_note-118)

### Christianity, feudalism, and Napoleonic wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Christianity, feudalism, and Napoleonic wars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Edouard_Castres-Bonaparte_au_St-Bernard_IMG_3221.jpg/250px-Edouard_Castres-Bonaparte_au_St-Bernard_IMG_3221.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edouard_Castres-Bonaparte_au_St-Bernard_IMG_3221.jpg)

Napoleon passing the Great St Bernard Pass, by [Edouard Castres](/wiki/Edouard_Castres "Edouard Castres")

Christianity was established in the Alps by the [Roman people](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people"). Monasteries and churches were constructed, even at high Alpine altitudes. The [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") expanded their [Carolingian Empire](/wiki/Carolingian_Empire "Carolingian Empire"), while the [Baiuvarii](/wiki/Baiuvarii "Baiuvarii") introduced [feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") in the eastern Alps. The construction of castles in the Alps supported the growing number of dukedoms and kingdoms. Castello del Buonconsiglio in [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento"), still has intricate frescoes, and excellent examples of [Gothic art](/wiki/Gothic_art "Gothic art"). The [Château de Chillon](/wiki/Ch%C3%A2teau_de_Chillon "Château de Chillon") is preserved as an example of medieval architecture.[[117]](#cite_note-119) There are several important alpine saints and one such one is [Saint Maurice](/wiki/Saint_Maurice "Saint Maurice").[[118]](#cite_note-120) Much of the medieval period was a time of power struggles between competing dynasties such as the [House of Savoy](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy"), the [Visconti of Milan](/wiki/Visconti_of_Milan "Visconti of Milan"), and the [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg").[[119]](#cite_note-121)

The [Great St Bernard Hospice](/wiki/Great_St_Bernard_Hospice "Great St Bernard Hospice"), built in the 9th or 10th centuries, at the summit of the [Great Saint Bernard Pass](/wiki/Great_Saint_Bernard_Pass "Great Saint Bernard Pass") was a shelter for humans and destination for pilgrims.[[120]](#cite_note-122) In 1291, to protect themselves from incursions by the House of Habsburg, four Alpine [cantons](/wiki/Cantons "Cantons") drew up the [Federal Charter of 1291](/wiki/Federal_Charter_of_1291 "Federal Charter of 1291"), which is considered to be a declaration of independence from neighbouring kingdoms. After a series of battles fought in the 13th, 14th, and 15th centuries, more cantons joined the confederacy and by the 16th century, [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland") was established as a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state").[[121]](#cite_note-123)

In the Alps, the [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession") fallout resulted in a 1713 [treaty](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty"), part of the [Peace of Utrecht](/wiki/Peace_of_Utrecht "Peace of Utrecht"), which relocated the Western Alps border along the watersheds. Historically, the Alps were used to determine the borders of political and administrative gangs, but the Peace of Utrecht was the first significant body of treaty that considered geographical conditions. The Alps were carved up and borders were agreed, so that [enclaves](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave") in the Alps could be eliminated.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

During the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars") in the late 18th century and early 19th century, [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") annexed territory formerly controlled by the House of Habsburg, and the House of Savoy. In 1798, the [Helvetic Republic](/wiki/Helvetic_Republic "Helvetic Republic") was established, two years later an army across the [Great St Bernard Pass](/wiki/Great_St_Bernard_Pass "Great St Bernard Pass").[[123]](#cite_note-125) In 1799 the Russian imperial military engaged the revolutionary French army in the Alps, this episode has been recorded as significant achievement in [mountain warfare](/wiki/Mountain_warfare "Mountain warfare").[[124]](#cite_note-126) In October 1799 the troops commanded by [Alexander Suvorov](/wiki/Alexander_Suvorov "Alexander Suvorov") were surrounded in the Alps by much larger French troops. The Russian troops broke out, mauled the French troops, and retreated through the [Panix Pass](/wiki/Panix_Pass "Panix Pass").[[125]](#cite_note-127)

After the fall of Napoleon, many alpine countries developed heavy protections to prevent further invasion. Thus, [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") built a series of fortifications to protect the major alpine passes, such as the [col du Mont-Cenis](/wiki/Col_du_Mont-Cenis "Col du Mont-Cenis"), which was crossed by [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") to obliterate the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards").

In the 19th century, the monasteries built in the Alps to shelter humans became tourist destinations. The [Benedictines](/wiki/Benedictine "Benedictine") had built monasteries in [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne"), and [Oberammergau](/wiki/Oberammergau "Oberammergau"). The [Cistercians](/wiki/Cistercian "Cistercian") built their temple at [Lake Constance](/wiki/Lake_Constance "Lake Constance"). Meanwhile, the [Augustinians](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians") maintained abbeys in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") and one in [Interlaken](/wiki/Interlaken "Interlaken").[[126]](#cite_note-128)

### Exploration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Exploration")]

Main article: [Exploration of the High Alps](/wiki/Exploration_of_the_High_Alps "Exploration of the High Alps")

[Radiocarbon](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating")-dated charcoal placed around 50,000 years ago was found in the *Drachloch* (Dragon's Hole) cave above the village of Vattis in the [canton of St. Gallen](/wiki/Canton_of_St._Gallen "Canton of St. Gallen"), proving that the high peaks were visited by prehistoric people. Seven bear skulls from the cave may have been buried by the same prehistoric people.[[127]](#cite_note-129) The peaks, however, were mostly ignored except for a few notable examples, and long left to the exclusive attention of the people of the adjoining valleys.[[128]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECoolidgeLakeKnox1911748-130)[[129]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff188ff-131) The mountain peaks were seen as terrifying, the abode of dragons and demons, to the point that people blindfolded themselves to cross the Alpine passes.[[130]](#cite_note-132) The glaciers remained a mystery and many still believed the highest areas to be inhabited by dragons.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

[Charles VII of France](/wiki/Charles_VII_of_France "Charles VII of France") ordered his [chamberlain](/wiki/Chamberlain_(office) "Chamberlain (office)") to climb [Mont Aiguille](/wiki/Mont_Aiguille "Mont Aiguille") in 1356. The knight reached the summit of [Rocciamelone](/wiki/Rocciamelone "Rocciamelone") where he left a bronze triptych of three crosses, a feat which he conducted with the use of ladders to traverse the ice.[[132]](#cite_note-134) In 1492, Antoine de Ville climbed Mont Aiguille, without reaching the summit, an experience he described as "horrifying and terrifying."[[129]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff188ff-131) [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci") was fascinated by variations of light in the higher altitudes, and climbed a mountain—scholars are uncertain which one; some believe it may have been [Monte Rosa](/wiki/Monte_Rosa "Monte Rosa"). From his description of a "blue like that of a gentian" sky it is thought that he reached a significantly high altitude.[[133]](#cite_note-135) In the 18th century four [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix") men almost made the summit of Mont Blanc but were overcome by altitude sickness and snowblindness.[[134]](#cite_note-136)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Descent_from_Mont-Blanc_in_1787.jpg/250px-Descent_from_Mont-Blanc_in_1787.jpg)](/wiki/File:Descent_from_Mont-Blanc_in_1787.jpg)

[Horace Bénédict de Saussure](/wiki/Horace_B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dict_de_Saussure "Horace Bénédict de Saussure") shown in, Descent from Mont-Blanc, by [Christian von Mechel](/wiki/Christian_von_Mechel "Christian von Mechel")

[Conrad Gessner](/wiki/Conrad_Gessner "Conrad Gessner") was the first naturalist to ascend the mountains in the 16th century, to study them, writing that in the mountains he found the "theatre of the Lord".[[135]](#cite_note-137) By the 19th century more naturalists began to arrive to explore, study and conquer the high peaks.[[136]](#cite_note-Fleming_vii-138) Two men who first explored the regions of ice and snow were [Horace-Bénédict de Saussure](/wiki/Horace-B%C3%A9n%C3%A9dict_de_Saussure "Horace-Bénédict de Saussure") (1740–1799) in the Pennine Alps,[[137]](#cite_note-139) and the Benedictine monk of [Disentis](/wiki/Disentis "Disentis") [Placidus a Spescha](/wiki/Placidus_a_Spescha "Placidus a Spescha") (1752–1833).[[136]](#cite_note-Fleming_vii-138) Born in Geneva, Saussure was enamoured with the mountains from an early age; he left a law career to become a naturalist and spent many years trekking through the Bernese Oberland, the Savoy, the Piedmont and Valais, studying the glaciers and geology, as he became an early proponent of the theory of rock upheaval.[[138]](#cite_note-140) Saussure, in 1787, was a member of the third ascent of Mont Blanc—today the summits of all the peaks have been climbed.[[35]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff197ff-37)

### The Romantics and Alpinists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: The Romantics and Alpinists")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg/250px-Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caspar_David_Friedrich_-_Wanderer_above_the_sea_of_fog.jpg)

*[Wanderer above the Sea of Fog](/wiki/Wanderer_above_the_Sea_of_Fog "Wanderer above the Sea of Fog")* by [Caspar David Friedrich](/wiki/Caspar_David_Friedrich "Caspar David Friedrich")

[Albrecht von Haller](/wiki/Albrecht_von_Haller "Albrecht von Haller")'s poem *Die Alpen*, published in 1732 described the mountains as an area of mythical purity.[[139]](#cite_note-141) [Jean-Jacques Rousseau](/wiki/Jean-Jacques_Rousseau "Jean-Jacques Rousseau") presented the Alps as a place of allure and beauty, in his novel *[Julie, or the New Heloise](/wiki/Julie,_or_the_New_Heloise "Julie, or the New Heloise")*, published in 1761. Later the first wave of [Romanticism](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") such as [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe"), and [J. M. W. Turner](/wiki/J._M._W._Turner "J. M. W. Turner") came to admire the Alpine scenery;[[140]](#cite_note-142) [Wordsworth](/wiki/William_Wordsworth "William Wordsworth") visited the area in 1790, writing of his experiences in *[The Prelude](/wiki/The_Prelude "The Prelude")* (1799). Schiller later wrote the play *[William Tell](/wiki/William_Tell_(play) "William Tell (play)")* (1804), which tells the story of the legendary Swiss marksman [William Tell](/wiki/William_Tell "William Tell") as part of the greater Swiss struggle for independence from the [Habsburg Empire](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") in the early 14th century. At the end of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), the Alpine countries began to see an influx of poets, artists, and musicians,[[141]](#cite_note-Fleming_2000,_83-143) as visitors came to experience the [sublime](/wiki/Sublime_(philosophy) "Sublime (philosophy)") effects of monumental nature.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

In 1816, [Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), [Percy Bysshe Shelley](/wiki/Percy_Bysshe_Shelley "Percy Bysshe Shelley") and his wife [Mary Shelley](/wiki/Mary_Shelley "Mary Shelley") visited Geneva and all three were inspired by the scenery in their writings.[[141]](#cite_note-Fleming_2000,_83-143) During these visits, Shelley wrote the poem "[Mont Blanc](/wiki/Mont_Blanc_(poem) "Mont Blanc (poem)")", Byron wrote "[The Prisoner of Chillon](/wiki/The_Prisoner_of_Chillon "The Prisoner of Chillon")" and the dramatic poem *[Manfred](/wiki/Manfred_(drama) "Manfred (drama)")*, and Mary Shelley, who found the scenery overwhelming, conceived the idea for the novel *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* in her villa on the shores of Lake Geneva amid a thunderstorm. When [Coleridge](/wiki/Samuel_Taylor_Coleridge "Samuel Taylor Coleridge") travelled to [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix"), he declaimed, in defiance of Shelley, who had signed himself "Atheos" in the guestbook of the Hotel de Londres near Montenvers,[[143]](#cite_note-145) "Who would be, who could be an atheist in this valley of wonders".[[144]](#cite_note-146)

By the mid-19th century, scientists began to arrive en masse to study the geology and ecology of the region.[[145]](#cite_note-147)

The tourism and mountaineering development of the Alps began at the beginning of the 19th century. In the early years of the [golden age of alpinism](/wiki/Golden_age_of_alpinism "Golden age of alpinism"), scientific activities were mixed with sport, for example by the physicist [John Tyndall](/wiki/John_Tyndall "John Tyndall"), with the first ascent of the Matterhorn by [Edward Whymper](/wiki/Edward_Whymper "Edward Whymper") being the highlight. In the later years, the [silver age of alpinism](/wiki/Silver_age_of_alpinism "Silver age of alpinism"), the focus was on mountain sports and climbing. The first president of the [Alpine Club](/wiki/Alpine_Club_(UK) "Alpine Club (UK)"), [John Ball](/wiki/John_Ball_(naturalist) "John Ball (naturalist)"), is considered the discoverer of the Dolomites, which for decades were the focus of climbers like [Paul Grohmann](/wiki/Paul_Grohmann "Paul Grohmann"), [Michael Innerkofler](/wiki/Michael_Innerkofler "Michael Innerkofler") and [Angelo Dibona](/wiki/Angelo_Dibona "Angelo Dibona").[[146]](#cite_note-148)[[147]](#cite_note-149)[[148]](#cite_note-150)

### The Nazis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: The Nazis")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Ghent_altarpiece_at_Altaussee.jpg/250px-Ghent_altarpiece_at_Altaussee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ghent_altarpiece_at_Altaussee.jpg)

The Nazis hid looted art in salt mines at [Altaussee](/wiki/Altaussee "Altaussee"), such as the [Early Netherlandish](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_art "Early Netherlandish art") *[Ghent Altarpiece](/wiki/Ghent_Altarpiece "Ghent Altarpiece")* which sustained significant damage.

In autumn 1932, [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") commissioned the first of a series of refurbishments, which eventually turned a mountain cottage, later named [Berghof](/wiki/Berghof_(residence) "Berghof (residence)"), into a fortified [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel"). This domestic, but representative, fortification had two small bedrooms, and a full bathroom, planned by the Munich architect and NSDAP member [Josef Neumaier](/w/index.php?title=Josef_Neumaier&action=edit&redlink=1 "Josef Neumaier (page does not exist)"). Guests, such as [Rudolf Hess](/wiki/Rudolf_Hess "Rudolf Hess"), stayed over, sleeping in tents or over the garage.[[149]](#cite_note-151)

The Alps, Adolf Hitler, and improbable powerful organisations have been subject to [crime fiction](/wiki/Crime_fiction "Crime fiction").[[150]](#cite_note-152) The Alps acted as a geographical barrier to [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), and the Alps for centuries were permeated with established smuggling routes, known as *green line*. After World War II, members of the [Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") that feared prosecution as [war criminals](/wiki/War_criminal "War criminal"), known in modern English only as *SS*, disappeared into a crowd of [refugees](/wiki/Refugee "Refugee").[[151]](#cite_note-153) Massive numbers of refugees entered Italy illegally, by navigating the Alps.[[152]](#cite_note-154)

There were [Ratlines](/wiki/Ratlines_(World_War_II) "Ratlines (World War II)") (i.e. one through Spain and the second through Rome and Genoa) used by German Nazis and other fascists to flee Europe from 1945 onwards.

### Undocumented migrants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Undocumented migrants")]

[Smugglers](/wiki/Smugglers "Smugglers") of humans have claimed that crossing the Alps is less dangerous, or deadly, than travelling 355 km on water between [Tripoli](/wiki/Tripoli,_Libya "Tripoli, Libya") and [Lampedusa](/wiki/Lampedusa "Lampedusa") with a [tramp ship](/wiki/Tramp_ship "Tramp ship") (*carretta del mare*) or a [dinghy](/wiki/Dinghy "Dinghy"). Some undocumented migrants, visa overstayers, false tourists, asylum seekers, and other clandestine humans, have died crossing the Alps. Their exact number can only be estimated.[[153]](#cite_note-155)
Today, people with smartphones can plan their travel routes, find their way around with Google Maps, and find information about travelling routes on social media.

### Largest Alpine cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Largest Alpine cities")]

The largest city within the Alps is the city of [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble") in France. Other larger and important cities within the Alps with over 100,000 inhabitants are in [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol "Tyrol") with [Bolzano/Bozen](/wiki/Bolzano "Bolzano") (Italy), [Trento](/wiki/Trento "Trento") (Italy), [Lucerne](/wiki/Lucerne "Lucerne") (Switzerland), and [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck") (Austria). Larger cities on the plains on its borders or with short access to the Alps are [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"), [Verona](/wiki/Verona "Verona"), [Brescia](/wiki/Brescia "Brescia") (Italy), [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") (Germany), [Graz](/wiki/Graz "Graz"), [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), [Linz](/wiki/Linz "Linz"), [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") (Austria), [Ljubljana](/wiki/Ljubljana "Ljubljana"), [Maribor](/wiki/Maribor "Maribor"), [Kranj](/wiki/Kranj "Kranj") (Slovenia), [Bern](/wiki/Bern "Bern"), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"), [Zurich](/wiki/Zurich "Zurich"), [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne"), [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva") (Switzerland), [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice"), [Lyon](/wiki/Lyon "Lyon") (France).

### Alpine people and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Alpine people and culture")]

Further information: [Transhumance in the Alps](/wiki/Transhumance_in_the_Alps "Transhumance in the Alps") and [Swiss folklore](/wiki/Swiss_folklore "Swiss folklore")

The population of the region is 14 million spread across eight countries.[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7) On the rim of the mountains, on the plateaus, and on the plains the economy consists of manufacturing and service jobs whereas in the higher altitudes and the mountains farming is still essential to the economy.[[154]](#cite_note-156) Farming and forestry continue to be mainstays of Alpine culture, industries that provide for export to the cities and maintain the mountain ecology.[[155]](#cite_note-Chartes_et._el._2010,_5-157)

The Alpine regions are multicultural and linguistically diverse. Dialects are common and vary from valley to valley and region to region. In the Slavic Alps alone 19 dialects have been identified. Some of the Romance dialects spoken in the French, Swiss and Italian alps of [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") derive from [Arpitan](/wiki/Arpitan "Arpitan"), while the southern part of the western range is related to [Occitan](/wiki/Occitan_language "Occitan language"); the German dialects derive from Germanic tribal languages.[[156]](#cite_note-Shoumataff_2001,_114–166-158) [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language"), spoken by two percent of the population in southeast Switzerland, is an ancient [Rhaeto-Romanic language](/wiki/Rhaeto-Romance_languages "Rhaeto-Romance languages") derived from Latin, remnants of ancient Celtic languages and perhaps [Etruscan](/wiki/Etruscan_language "Etruscan language").[[156]](#cite_note-Shoumataff_2001,_114–166-158)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Hallstatt_-_Nordansicht.JPG/250px-Hallstatt_-_Nordansicht.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hallstatt_-_Nordansicht.JPG)

[Hallstatt](/wiki/Hallstatt "Hallstatt") is known for its production of salt, dating back to prehistoric times ([Hallstatt culture](/wiki/Hallstatt_culture "Hallstatt culture"))

Much of the Alpine culture is unchanged since hundreds of years when skills that guaranteed survival in the mountain valleys and the highest villages became mainstays, leading to strong traditions of carpentry, woodcarving, baking, pastry-making, and cheesemaking.[[157]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff123ff-159)

Farming has been a traditional occupation for centuries, although it became less dominant in the 20th century with the advent of tourism. Grazing and pasture land are limited because of the steep and rocky topography of the Alps. In mid-June, cows are moved to the highest pastures close to the snowline, where they are watched by herdsmen who stay in the high altitudes often living in stone huts or wooden barns during the summers.[[157]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff123ff-159) Villagers celebrate the day the cows are herded up to the pastures and again when they return in mid-September. The [Almabtrieb, Alpabzug, Alpabfahrt, Désalpes](/wiki/Almabtrieb "Almabtrieb") ("coming down from the alps") is celebrated by decorating the cows with garlands and enormous [cowbells](/wiki/Cowbell "Cowbell") while the farmers dress in traditional costumes.[[157]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff123ff-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/LeMonal-fontaine-chapelle-Savoie.JPG/250px-LeMonal-fontaine-chapelle-Savoie.JPG)](/wiki/File:LeMonal-fontaine-chapelle-Savoie.JPG)

In the summers the cows are brought up to the high mountain meadows for grazing. Small summer villages such as the one shown in this photograph taken in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") are used.

Cheesemaking is an ancient tradition in most Alpine countries. A wheel of cheese from the [Emmental](/wiki/Emmental "Emmental") in Switzerland can weigh up to 45 kg (100 lb), and the [Beaufort](/wiki/Beaufort_cheese "Beaufort cheese") in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy") can weigh up to 70 kg (150 lb). Owners of the cows traditionally receive from the cheesemakers a portion about the proportion of the cows' milk from the summer months in the high alps. Haymaking is an important farming activity in mountain villages that have become somewhat mechanised in recent years, although the slopes are so steep that scythes are usually necessary to cut the grass. Hay is normally brought in twice a year, often also on festival days.[[157]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff123ff-159)

In the high villages, people live in homes built according to medieval designs that withstand cold winters. The kitchen is separated from the living area (called the *stube*, the area of the home heated by a stove), and second-floor bedrooms benefit from rising heat. The typical Swiss [chalet](/wiki/Chalet "Chalet") originated in the [Bernese Oberland](/wiki/Bernese_Oberland "Bernese Oberland"). Chalets often face south or downhill and are built of solid wood, with a steeply gabled roof to allow accumulated snow to slide off easily. Stairs leading to upper levels are sometimes built on the outside, and balconies are sometimes enclosed.[[157]](#cite_note-Shoumatoff123ff-159)[[158]](#cite_note-160)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Schaufschod_2009_05.JPG/250px-Schaufschod_2009_05.JPG)](/wiki/File:Schaufschod_2009_05.JPG)

Herding sheep in Austria

Food is passed from the kitchen to the stube, where the dining room table is placed. Some meals are communal, such as [fondue](/wiki/Fondue "Fondue"), where a pot is set in the middle of the table for each person to dip into. Other meals are still served traditionally on carved wooden plates. Furniture has been traditionally elaborately carved and in many Alpine countries, carpentry skills are passed from generation to generation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Toit_de_Lauzes.jpg/250px-Toit_de_Lauzes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toit_de_Lauzes.jpg)

Alpine chalet being built in Haute-[Maurienne](/wiki/Maurienne "Maurienne") (Savoy). The use of thick pieces of [orthogneiss](/wiki/Orthogneiss "Orthogneiss") (4–7 cm) is by the strict architectural regulations in the region bordering the national parks of Vanoise-Grand Paradis.

Roofs are traditionally constructed from Alpine rocks such as pieces of [schist](/wiki/Schist "Schist"), gneiss, or [slate](/wiki/Slate "Slate").[[159]](#cite_note-161) Such chalets are typically found in the higher parts of the valleys, as in the [Maurienne](/wiki/Maurienne "Maurienne") valley in [Savoy](/wiki/Savoy "Savoy"), where the amount of snow during the cold months is important. The inclination of the roof cannot exceed 40%, allowing the snow to stay on top, thereby functioning as insulation from the cold.[[160]](#cite_note-162) In the lower areas where the forests are widespread, wooden tiles are traditionally used. Commonly made of [Norway spruce](/wiki/Norway_spruce "Norway spruce"), they are called "tavaillon".

In the German-speaking parts of the Alps ([Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland")) and also [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia"), there is a strong tradition of Alpine [folk culture](/wiki/Folk_culture "Folk culture"). Old traditions are carefully maintained among inhabitants of Alpine areas, even though this is seldom obvious to the visitor: many people are members of cultural associations where the Alpine folk culture is cultivated. At cultural events, traditional [folk costume](/wiki/Folk_costume "Folk costume") (in German *[Tracht](/wiki/Tracht "Tracht")*) is expected: typically [lederhosen](/wiki/Lederhosen "Lederhosen") for men and [dirndls](/wiki/Dirndl "Dirndl") for women. Visitors can get a glimpse of the rich customs of the Alps at public [Volksfeste](/wiki/Volksfest "Volksfest"). Even when large events feature only a little folk culture, all participants take part with gusto. Good opportunities to see local people celebrating the traditional culture occur at the many fairs, wine festivals, and firefighting festivals which fill weekends in the countryside from spring to autumn. Alpine festivals vary from country to country. Frequently they include music (e.g. the playing of [Alpenhorns](/wiki/Alpenhorn "Alpenhorn")), dance (e.g. [Schuhplattler](/wiki/Schuhplattler "Schuhplattler")), sports (e.g. wrestling marches and archery), as well as traditions with pagan roots such as the lighting of fires on [Walpurgis Night](/wiki/Walpurgis_Night "Walpurgis Night") and [Saint John's Eve](/wiki/Saint_John%27s_Eve "Saint John's Eve"). Many areas celebrate [Fastnacht](/wiki/Swabian-Alemannic_Fastnacht "Swabian-Alemannic Fastnacht") in the weeks before [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"). Folk costume also continues to be worn for most weddings and festivals.[[161]](#cite_note-163)[[162]](#cite_note-marcopolo1-164)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Further information: [List of national parks in the Alps](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_in_the_Alps "List of national parks in the Alps"), [Tourism in Austria](/wiki/Tourism_in_Austria "Tourism in Austria"), [Tourism in France](/wiki/Tourism_in_France "Tourism in France"), [Tourism in Germany](/wiki/Tourism_in_Germany "Tourism in Germany"), [Tourism in Italy](/wiki/Tourism_in_Italy "Tourism in Italy"), and [Tourism in Switzerland](/wiki/Tourism_in_Switzerland "Tourism in Switzerland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Karl_Schranz%2C_Lauberhorn_1966.jpg/250px-Karl_Schranz%2C_Lauberhorn_1966.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karl_Schranz,_Lauberhorn_1966.jpg)

[Karl Schranz](/wiki/Karl_Schranz "Karl Schranz") running the [Lauberhorn](/wiki/Lauberhorn "Lauberhorn") in 1966

The Alps are one of the more popular tourist destinations in the world with many resorts such as [Oberstdorf](/wiki/Oberstdorf "Oberstdorf"), in Bavaria, [Saalbach](/wiki/Saalbach-Hinterglemm "Saalbach-Hinterglemm") in Austria, [Davos](/wiki/Davos "Davos") in Switzerland, [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix") in France, and [Cortina d'Ampezzo](/wiki/Cortina_d%27Ampezzo "Cortina d'Ampezzo") in Italy recording more than a million annual visitors. With over 120 million visitors a year, tourism is integral to the Alpine economy with much of it coming from winter sports, although summer visitors are also an important component.[[163]](#cite_note-Bart-165)

The tourism industry began in the early 19th century when foreigners visited the Alps, travelled to the bases of the mountains to enjoy the scenery, and stayed at the spa-resorts. Large hotels were built during the [Belle Époque](/wiki/Belle_%C3%89poque "Belle Époque"); [cog-railways](/wiki/Rack_railway "Rack railway"), built early in the 20th century, brought tourists to ever-higher elevations, with the [Jungfraubahn](/wiki/Jungfraubahn "Jungfraubahn") terminating at the Jungfraujoch, well above the eternal snow-line, after going through a tunnel in Eiger. During this period winter sports were slowly introduced: in 1882 the first [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating "Figure skating") championship was held in [St. Moritz](/wiki/St._Moritz "St. Moritz"), and downhill skiing became a popular sport with English visitors early in the 20th century,[[163]](#cite_note-Bart-165) as the first ski-lift was installed in 1908 above Grindelwald.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

In the first half of the 20th century the Olympic Winter Games were held three times in Alpine venues: the [1924 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") in Chamonix, France; the [1928 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1928_Winter_Olympics "1928 Winter Olympics") in St. Moritz, Switzerland; and the [1936 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1936_Winter_Olympics "1936 Winter Olympics") in [Garmisch-Partenkirchen](/wiki/Garmisch-Partenkirchen "Garmisch-Partenkirchen"), Germany. During World War II the winter games were cancelled but after that time the Winter Games have been held in [St. Moritz (1948)](/wiki/1948_Winter_Olympics "1948 Winter Olympics"), [Cortina d'Ampezzo (1956)](/wiki/1956_Winter_Olympics "1956 Winter Olympics"), [Innsbruck](/wiki/Innsbruck "Innsbruck"), Austria (1964 and 1976), [Grenoble](/wiki/Grenoble "Grenoble"), France, (1968), [Albertville](/wiki/Albertville "Albertville"), France, (1992), and [Turin (2006)](/wiki/2006_Winter_Olympics "2006 Winter Olympics").[[165]](#cite_note-167) In 1930, the *Lauberhorn Rennen* (Lauberhorn Race), was run for the first time on the [Lauberhorn](/wiki/Lauberhorn "Lauberhorn") above [Wengen](/wiki/Wengen "Wengen");[[166]](#cite_note-168) the equally demanding [Hahnenkamm](/wiki/Hahnenkamm,_Kitzb%C3%BChel "Hahnenkamm, Kitzbühel") was first run in the same year in [Kitzbühel](/wiki/Kitzb%C3%BChel "Kitzbühel"), Austria.[[167]](#cite_note-169) Both races continue to be held each January on successive weekends. The Lauberhorn is the more strenuous downhill race at 4.5 km (2.8 mi) and poses danger to racers who reach 130 km/h (81 mph) within seconds of leaving the start gate.[[168]](#cite_note-170)

During the post-World War I period, ski lifts were built in Swiss and Austrian towns to accommodate winter visitors, and summer tourism continued to be important. By the mid-20th century the popularity of downhill skiing increased greatly as it became more accessible and in the 1970s several new villages were built in France devoted almost exclusively to skiing, such as [Les Menuires](/wiki/Les_Menuires "Les Menuires"). Until this point, Austria and Switzerland had been the traditional and more popular destinations for winter sports, and by the end of the 20th century and into the early 21st century, France, Italy, and Tyrol began to see increases in winter visitors.[[163]](#cite_note-Bart-165) Since the 1980s tourism expansion and easy global access generate grave concerns regarding the loss of traditional Alpine culture and many uncertainties about sustainable development.[[169]](#cite_note-171) As a likely result of climatic change, the number of high altitude ski resorts and piste km is in decline since 2015, with snow-making machines installed at many sites.[[170]](#cite_note-172)

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Alps&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/ZB_Interregio_mit_HGe_101_bei_Niederried.jpg/250px-ZB_Interregio_mit_HGe_101_bei_Niederried.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZB_Interregio_mit_HGe_101_bei_Niederried.jpg)

[Zentralbahn](/wiki/Zentralbahn "Zentralbahn") Interregio train following the [Lake Brienz](/wiki/Lake_Brienz "Lake Brienz") shoreline, near [Niederried](/wiki/Niederried_bei_Interlaken "Niederried bei Interlaken") in Switzerland

The region is serviced by 4,200 km (2,600 mi) of roads used by six million vehicles per year.[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7) Train travel is well established in the Alps, with, for instance 120 km (75 mi) of track for every 1,000 km2 (390 sq mi) in a country such as Switzerland.[[171]](#cite_note-173) Most of [Europe's highest railways](/wiki/List_of_highest_railways_in_Europe "List of highest railways in Europe") are located there. In 2007, the new 34.57 km-long (21.48 mi) [Lötschberg Base Tunnel](/wiki/L%C3%B6tschberg_Base_Tunnel "Lötschberg Base Tunnel") was opened, which circumvents the 100 years older [Lötschberg Tunnel](/wiki/L%C3%B6tschberg_Tunnel "Lötschberg Tunnel"). With the opening of the 57.1 km-long (35.5 mi) [Gotthard Base Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Base_Tunnel "Gotthard Base Tunnel") on 1 June 2016, it bypasses the [Gotthard Tunnel](/wiki/Gotthard_Tunnel "Gotthard Tunnel") built in the 19th century and realises the first flat route through the Alps.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

Some high mountain villages are [car-free](/wiki/Pedestrian_zone "Pedestrian zone") either because of inaccessibility or by choice. [Wengen](/wiki/Wengen "Wengen"), and [Zermatt](/wiki/Zermatt "Zermatt") (in Switzerland) are accessible only by [cable car](/wiki/Aerial_lift "Aerial lift") or [cog-rail trains](/wiki/Rack_railway "Rack railway"). [Avoriaz](/wiki/Avoriaz "Avoriaz") (in France), is car-free, with other Alpine villages considering becoming car-free zones or limiting the number of cars for reasons of sustainability of the fragile Alpine terrain.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

The lower regions and larger towns of the Alps are well-served by motorways and main roads, but higher mountain passes and byroads, which are amongst the [highest in Europe](/wiki/List_of_highest_paved_roads_in_Europe "List of highest paved roads in Europe"), can be treacherous even in summer due to steep slopes. Many passes are closed in winter. Several airports around the Alps (and some within), as well as long-distance rail links from all neighbouring countries, afford large numbers of travellers easy access.[[5]](#cite_note-Chatré8-7)
